review_summary: Textual grounding is sufficient for concept-focused questions, and no separate video clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What additional memory does ZeRO stage 2 save compared with ZeRO stage 1?
choices: It shards gradients so the full gradient vector does not need to live on every GPU at once.
It removes all activations from memory during the forward pass.
It stores model parameters only on the CPU and never communicates them.
It freezes all parameters, so no optimizer is needed.
question_bank.explanation: Stage 1 shards optimizer state. Stage 2 goes further by sharding gradient storage, often sending layer gradients to their owning workers as they are produced.

transcript_quotes: send it to the right worker

question_bank.question: What distinguishes ZeRO stage 3, also called FSDP, from earlier ZeRO stages?
choices: It shards the model parameters themselves in addition to optimizer state and gradients.
It stops using multiple GPUs and moves back to single-GPU training.
It keeps all parameters replicated but compresses only the input data.
It removes the need for backpropagation by using forward-only updates.
question_bank.explanation: FSDP targets cases where the model itself may not fit on one GPU. By sharding parameters, no worker has to permanently store the full model.

question_bank.question: During an FSDP forward pass, why is an all-gather needed before computing a layer?
choices: Because each GPU has only a shard of that layer's parameters, so the full layer parameters must be temporarily reconstructed for computation.
Because each GPU has a different vocabulary and must merge tokenizers before every layer.
Because all activations must be averaged before any layer can run.
Because FSDP requires every layer to use the same random seed.
question_bank.explanation: Parameter sharding saves memory, but a layer computation still needs the layer's full weights. FSDP temporarily gathers those weights, computes the layer, and can then free them.

question_bank.question: Why does activation memory remain a separate bottleneck even when parameters and optimizer state are sharded?
choices: Activations are saved for backpropagation and scale with batch size, so sharding model state does not eliminate them.
Activations are stored permanently in the optimizer state, so ZeRO cannot access them.
Activations are always FP64, regardless of mixed precision.
Activations are only used during inference, not training.
question_bank.explanation: Sharding reduces memory for parameters, gradients, and optimizer state. The intermediate values needed to compute gradients still occupy memory and grow with the batch and sequence workload.
